title: FINS3640 Lecture4
body:
Advanced Topics in Corporate Bond Pricing
Lecture 4
 Course Outline

 Pricing plain vanilla corporate bonds
     Risk premium in corporate bonds: Credit spreads

 Binomial trees and the modeling of yield uncertainties
 Callable corporate bonds
     Definition
     Why do we observe callable bonds in practice?
     Theory of pricing

 Puttable corporate bonds
     Definition
     Why do we observe puttable bonds in practice?
     Theory of pricing



                                                FINS 3640
 Determinants of credit spreads in corporate bonds

      • The reference rate: the minimum rate that investors can expect to get for a
        given maturity. Note that there is a reference rate for each maturity.


      • The reference rate corresponds to the cost of borrowing of the least risky
        issuer in the market: Usually governments
           • Example: Rate of US Treasuries for USD bonds


      • For a given maturity and a given currency, this rate is the YTM of bonds
        issued by government, which is « on-the-run », that is the last bonds that
        have been issued through an auction process.




3                                         FINS 3640                                   3
 Determinants of credit spreads in corporate bonds

     • All bonds (except those of the govt.) are traded with a spread relative to the
       government bonds with similar maturity which defined the reference rate for a
       given maturity.
     • This is called credit spread


       Example
         • YTM of a 10-year bond of Australia: 3.462%
         • YTM of a 10-year bond of Australian Gas Networks: 7.476%
         • Credit Spread: 7.476% - 3.462% = 4.014% or 401 basis points
         • Requirements for comparability: bonds must have similar characteristics
           (T & CR).




4                                        FINS 3640                                      4
 Determinants of credit spreads in corporate bonds
      • Where does this spread comes from?
         • It's a risk premium


      • What factors influence mostly the spread?
         • Credit risk (default risk) – the probability that a given issuer is unable to
           repay its engagements (FV and C).
         • Liquidity of the bond – Certain bonds are more liquid than others.


      • In general, the higher the default risk the higher the spread is and the less
        liquid a bond is the higher the spread is.

      • Government bonds are often more liquid than corporate bonds.
5                                         FINS 3640                                        5
 Determinants of credit spreads in corporate bonds
                 Risk premium of corporations in US, 1997-2013

                                                                                            FINS 3640                                               8
                                                                                                                                                            8
 Callable bonds

• The issuer of a callable bond has the right, but not the obligation, to
  repurchase bonds in the future at a fixed price determined in advance
• Why would a firm issue callable bonds? To benefit from
   • Decrease of interest rates in the future
   • Improved credit risk and therefore a decrease in the YTM: If the credit
     rating of the company is upgraded, for example
• So the firm will take the possibility to benefit from more advantageous
  financing rates in the future
• For the lender:
    • Reinvestment risk
    • Thus, the price paid for a callable will be lower than for a similar bond
       with no callable feature.




                                     FINS 3640                                    9
 Callable bonds

 Specifications of a callable bond:

 1.   The repurchase price (prior to maturity): Call price
      This price is often equivalent to the nominal value + an annual coupon
      payment and may vary over time (note: we assume it fixed in this course)

 2.   The date on which the bond can be repurchased:
      Often there is a protection period of callable and therefore we talk about
      deferred bonds (i.e. may be callable only from a specific date)




                                      FINS 3640                                    10
 Callable bonds

How the callable bonds benefit from a lower rates?
  • ABC Company needs $ 100M today. It issues callable (CR=10%, repurchase
     price 105):
       •   This will cost $ 10 million per year to the company (coupons)


  •   2 years later, the rates are 8%:
       •   Companies borrow at the moment pay less than ABC


  •   The company wants to buy its «old» expensive debt
       •   The Company may issue new debt at 8%
       •   Even if the old debt is to be repurchased at $ 105M (call price), this is still
           optimal for the company:

                          The new cost = 8% x 105M$ = 8.4M$ (vs 10M$)



                                         FINS 3640                                           11
 Callable Bonds: price calculation
       • Suppose a 2-year maturity bond with annual coupon of 10% and callable in
         one year at $ 1,040
         • Suppose the required yield by investors is 5% and remains constant (so
           we know the spot rate of 1 year in the coming year)

         • One can confirm with certainty that the bond will be called next year
           because:


         • So what is the value of the bond today?




                                         FINS 3640                                  12
 Callable Bonds: price calculation

• But what if we are uncertain, even very slightly, regarding the future interest
  rates?
• When we compare a callable bond and a conventional bond, two things are
  important :
    • For very high yields: the probability that the callable bond will be repurchased
      is very small (why would the issuing company refinance at a higher rate?). So
      the two bonds will have similar price
    • For very low yields: the probability that the callable bond will be repurchased is
      very large (the issuing company will want to take advantage of the possibility to
      refinance at a lower rate). So the callable bond will be worth less than
      conventional bond

components of the price of the bond

                                      FINS 3640                                                      15
 Callable Bonds:
components of the price of the bond
• The former graph shows that the price of a callable bond is therefore
  composed of two elements :
    1. The price of a non callable bond
    2. The value of the right of the issuer to repurchase the bond at the
        callable price

• The latter value is the value of holding a call option on the bond
    • This call option is in the hands of the issuer

• So we have:
                              𝑉𝑉𝐶𝐶,0 = 𝑉𝑉𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁,0 − 𝑶𝑶𝑶𝑶𝑶𝑶𝟎𝟎




                                      FINS 3640                             16
 Callable Bonds: callable option

      • What affects the value of this option?
         • Uncertainty about future bond price

      • Where comes the uncertainty in the future price of bonds?
         • Uncertainty with respect to future interest rates

      • Which rate are we talking about?
          Future spot rate (forward rate)

      • So we need a model that takes into account the rates in the future and their
        uncertainty (or volatility)




                                           FINS 3640                                   17
     A binomial model to price callable bonds


• Suppose rt represents the sum of 1-
  year spot rate and credit spread in
  period t

• How to illustrate the uncertainty of r1?              V1,H+C       Cash flow
                                                        r1,H         if up (+)
   One approach is to assume that r1
    can take 2 values: H or L
   There will be a certain probability      V0    rate if up (+)
    (p) and (1-p) for each value             r0
   r1,H : the sum of government one-
    year spot rate and credit spread in                               Cash flow
                                                         V1,L +C      if down (-)
    the up state.
                                                         r1,L
   r1,L : the sum of government one-
    year spot rate and credit spread in
    the low state.                                rate if down (-)

                                      FINS 3640                                     18
  A binomial model to price callable bonds

• How to price of the callable bond?
    • We can calculate cash flow associated with each node by discounting
      the value at maturity with the appropriate forward rate(r1,H and r1,L)
        • 𝑉𝑉1,𝐻𝐻 is calculated by discounting the value at maturity with the
          forward rate r1,H whereas 𝑉𝑉1,𝐿𝐿 is calculated by using the forward
          rate r1,L

    • 𝑉𝑉0 is therefore the weighted average of these two values to be
      discounted again, but this time with r0:


                               𝑉𝑉1,𝐻𝐻 + 𝐶𝐶          𝑉𝑉1,𝐿𝐿 + 𝐶𝐶
                    𝑉𝑉0 = 𝑝𝑝               + 1 − 𝑝𝑝
                                 1 + 𝑟𝑟0             1 + 𝑟𝑟0


                                         FINS 3640                              19
   A binomial model to price callable bonds

• Example: Take a 2 years maturity bond with coupon rate of 10%, callable at $
  1,040 in one period (same bond as before)
    • Probability 50/50 of the future 1-year spot rate of 4% or 6%



• What is the value of a non callable bond?
   • At time 1, the two possible values are:
                                1100$                         1100$
                     𝑉𝑉1,𝐻𝐻 =         = 1037.74$ and 𝑉𝑉1,𝐿𝐿 =       = 1057.69$
                                 1.06                          1.04

    • Replacing in the formula we get:

                          1037.74$ + 100$          1057.69$ + 100$
          𝑉𝑉𝑁𝑁𝐶𝐶,0 = 𝑝𝑝                   + 1 − 𝑝𝑝                 = 1093.06$
                                1.05                     1.05



                                               FINS 3640                         20
   A binomial model to price callable bonds

• What is the price of the callable bond?
        • Case H: the value remains the same
           Case L: profitable for the issuer to repurchase the bond
    • The value would be:
                              min(𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶; 𝑽𝑽𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯 ) + 100$          min(𝑪𝑪𝑪𝑪; 𝑉𝑉1,𝐿𝐿 ) + 100$
                𝑉𝑉𝐶𝐶,0 = 𝑝𝑝                              + 1 − 𝑝𝑝                           = 1084.64$
                                        1.05                               1.05
                              𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏.𝟕𝟕𝟕𝟕𝟕+100$              𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏+100$
                     = 𝑝𝑝                         +   1 − 𝑝𝑝                  = 1084.64$
                                       1.05                          1.05

    • We can calculate the value of the option:
                   𝑂𝑂𝑂𝑂𝑂𝑂0 = 𝑉𝑉𝑁𝑁𝑁𝑁,0 − 𝑉𝑉𝐶𝐶,0 = 1093.06$ − 1084.64$ = 8.42$

• It is also possible to provide the value of the option in basis points (i.e. by
  comparing the YTM of these two obligations).



                                                        FINS 3640                                        21
   A binomial model to price callable bonds

• The binomial tree of interest rates
    • Allows to model the volatility of interest rates (required to assess callable
      bonds)
    • It is the change in forward interest rates over time for a given level of
      volatility

                                                    r3HHH
                                                    NHHH
                                      r2HH
                                      NHH
                        r1H                         r3HHL
                        NH                          NHHL
            ro                        r2HL
            N                         NHL           r3HLL
                        r1L
                                                    NHLL
                        NL
                                      r2LL
                                      NLL            r3LLL
                                                     NLLL
                                     FINS 3640                                        22
   A binomial model to price callable bonds


• Features of the binomial tree:
    • Each node is represented by N
    • H represents the higher of two future spot rates
    • L represents the lower of two future spot rates
    • At the first node of the tree, N, is the current 1-year spot rate, the spot
      rate of the first period (i.e., the first year).
        • It is the only known rate, r0




                                     FINS 3640                                      23
   A binomial model to price callable bonds

• What properties of a binomial tree should have?
   • First, it should reflect all available information
        • The variation of spot rate must match what is empirically observed
        • We use the Greek symbol sigma (σ) to denote the volatility
    • Then, the tree has to give the «right» prices for those non-callable bonds
        • The prices calculated for bonds must equal to those observed in
          markets
        • If this is the case, we say that the tree is arbitrage free and is
          properly calibrated




                                   FINS 3640                                       24
   A binomial model to evaluate callable bonds


• The change in interest rates according to the binomial tree:
    • It is assumed that the forward rates follow a lognormal random walk
    • σ: volatility of a hypothetical 1-year forward rates
    • r1,L: Low rate in one year
    • r1,H: High rate in one year

• With a given r1,L, we calculate r1,H as follow: r1,H= r1,L(e2σ)
   • It can be shown that the volatility σ corresponds to the volatility of the 1
      year spot rate.




                                    FINS 3640                                       25
   A binomial model to price callable bonds

• So how to determine r1,L?
    • By minimization in any calculation software like Excel, Python, etc…
    • Otherwise through trial and error, we build the tree so that it reproduces the observed
      prices of non-callable bonds

• Steps:
    • Step 1: Choose a non-callable bond of 2 years of the same issuer. Price it using the value of
      r1,L assumed
                              𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹+𝐶𝐶                       𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹+𝐶𝐶
           𝑽𝑽𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯 =              σ and 𝑽𝑽𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟏𝟏,𝑳𝑳 =
                            1+r1,L𝑒𝑒 2                      1+r1,L
                            𝑽𝑽       +𝐶𝐶                𝑽𝑽𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟏𝟏,𝑳𝑳 +𝐶𝐶
           𝑽𝑽𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟎𝟎 = 𝑝𝑝 𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯       + 1 − 𝑝𝑝                        compare 𝑽𝑽𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟎𝟎 to market price and adjust r1,L if not matched.
                            1+r0                            1+r0
    • Step 2: Choose a non-callable obligation with 3 years. Taking the values of r0 and r1,L, make
      a guess for r2,L and price the bond. Adjust r2,L until the model price matches the observed
      market price.
    • Steps 3, 4 …: Repeat these steps with bonds of increasing maturity to find r3,L r4,L etc… for
      all years needed.
• These steps constitute the calibration of the tree
    • When calibration is complete, we can price any bonds from the same issuer.. callable or
      not
                                                               FINS 3640                                                                       26
 Construction of the binomial tree: first trial
                        Maturity             YTM (%)                Price
                        (years)
                            1                  3.50                 $100
                            2                  4.00                 $100
                            3                  4.50                 $100


        Above are the bonds currently available for a given issuer & trading at par
        Hypothetical volatility of 1 year spot rates: σ = 10%
        Application 1: Using the information in the table, find r1,L.




                                             FINS 3640                                 27
                 Application 1: Construction of the binomial tree - r1,L
• Using the information in the previous table, find r1,L.




• 1: Choose a value for r1L, the one-year spot rate in one year in the low state (if rates decrease). For the first
  test, we choose 4.5%.
• 2: Determine r1,H, the one-year spot rate in one year in the high state (if rates increase) is then r1,H= r1,L(e2σ) =
  4.50%(e2x0.10)
• 3: Calculate the value (price) of the bond in one year for each node (the scenarios high rates and low rates)
• 4: Calculate the present value (price) by discounting the two values calculated in step 3 given r0
• 5: Compare the present value calculated in step 4 to the current market value of 100$



• see Excel Lecture 4 for answers.



                                                            FINS 3640                                               28
                                                                                                                          28
              Application 1: Construction of the binomial tree - r1,L

𝑽𝑽𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟎𝟎 = 𝑝𝑝 𝑵𝑵𝑪𝑪,𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯     + 1 − 𝑝𝑝                      .
                 1+r0                        1+r0

                                                                    C = 4.00

                                   V0 = 99.567
                                   r0 = 3.5%                                                                  C = 4.00
                                                                    V1,L = 99.522
                                                                    C = 4.00
                                                                    r1,L = 4.500%                             V2,LL = 100
                                                                                                              C = 4.00


                                                                                                             𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹 + 𝐶𝐶

                                                                                                             1 + r1,L

                                                                                                                            29
 Application 1: Construction of the binomial tree - r1,L

• In the first trial, 4.5% was selected (randomly) as the 1-year spot rates in the
  low state (r1,L )
• The calculated present value of $ 99.567 <$ 100, the market value
• So we can say that the r1,L that we choose is too high (price too low:
  Relationship #1 between price and YTM). We must repeat the calculation
  steps by choosing a slightly lower rate so that the price will be higher.
• Suppose that after several attempts, we finally choose 4.074%.
• We can see from Excel Lecture 4 – Application 1 that the price obtained with
  the tree is equal to the market ($ 100)
• The tree is then well calibrated for the first time-step (r1,L & r1,H ).




                                                                                     30
 Application 1: Construction of the binomial tree - r1,L


                                       V2,HH = 100
                                       C = 4.00
                       V1,H = 99.070
                       C = 4.00
        V0 = 100       r1,H = 4.976%   V2,HL =100
        r0 = 3.5%                      C = 4.00
                       V1,L = 99.929
                       C = 4.00
                       r1,L = 4.074%   V2,LL = 100
                                       C = 4.00




                         FINS 3640                        31
 Extension of the binomial tree: r2,LL
       • Suppose that we want to now expand the binomial tree and add a third year
       • We must now use the non-callable bond with term of 3 years. After some
         tests, we find that r2,LL = 4.53% for the 3rd year (i.e., V0 = Market price).



                                                                 V2,HH = 97.886          FV = 100
                                                                 C = 4.50                C = 4.50
                                         V1,H = 98.074           r2,HH= 6.757%
                                         C = 4.50
                 V0 = 100                r1,H = 4.976%           V2,HL=99.022
                 r0 = 3.5%                                                               FV = 100
                                                                 C = 4.50                C = 4.50

                                         C = 4.50
                                         r1,L = 4.074%           V2,LL = 99.972
                                                                                         FV = 100
                                                                 C = 4.50
                                                                                         C = 4.50
                                                                 r2,LL= 4.530%

 Application 2: Pricing of a non-callable bond by using the binomial
tree

    maturity or less of the same issuer.
  • Question: Using the tree calibrated from previous steps, what is the price of a
    3-year maturity non-callable bond, coupon rate of 5.25%?

 Pricing of a callable bond by using the binomial tree

  • Recall that: The value of the callable option = value of the non-callable bond -
    The value of the callable bond

                                       FINS 3640                                       37
 Callable bonds: Yield to call

• For callable bonds, it may not be appropriate to calculate the yield to maturity
    • The reason is that the bond may be repurchased before maturity (so we do not
      know the exact date at which it may be call).
    • YTM is a good indicator of the realized yield on a bond only if the bond is held to
      maturity and if the level of interest rates do not change.
• Investors use instead what is named the yield to call
    • This yield is calculated by assuming that the bond is held until the callable date
      and that it will be call (bought back by the issuer) on that date.
• As with the yield to maturity, yield to call assumes coupons are reinvested at
  this yield between today and the first callable date.
    • If the bond is sold at a premium, the call can be anticipated and therefore the
      yield to call is an important measure




                                        FINS 3640                                           38
 Callable bonds: Yield to call
                                                                     Callable
                                                                      bond
• Example: a 30-year maturity callable bond
  with annual payments has a coupon rate of            Issue        01/01/2000
  8%, and is callable in 10 years at the price of    Maturity       01/01/2030
  1080$.
                                                      Coupon           8%
                                                     Nominal
• What is the yield to call?                          Value           1000$

    • We only take the first date on which the       Call price       1080$
      obligation is callable as maturity
                                                    Protection      01/01/2010
    • This gives a yield of 6.52%
                                                       Price          1150$
    • In this example, we see that the yield to
      call is smaller than yield to maturity            YTM           6.82%

                                                    Yield to Call     6.52%




                                     FINS 3640                                   39
 Callable bonds: Yield to worst

• In practice, investors also calculate what we name: yield to worst
    • The yield to worst is the minimum of all the yield to call for each potential
      date of the repurchase.
        • Suppose we have a callable bond with 5 years to maturity and callable after 2 years.
          Then, we have can compute the yield to call for year 2, 3, and 4. Each potential date of
          repurchase gives us a corresponding yield to call.
        • The yield to worst is the minimum of all the yield to worst and the yield to maturity.


    • The yield to worst performance is traditionally used by investors to analyze
      the attractiveness of callable bond (i.e., worst case scenario).
        • Purpose: To compare different callable obligations




                                           FINS 3640                                                 40
 Puttable bonds

• Puttable bonds, conversely, gives the bondholder the option to sell the bond
  back to the issuer before maturity:
    • The resale price (or put price - PP) is determined at the bond issuance.
    • This is an advantageous feature for the bondholder
    • Thus, puttable bonds (𝑉𝑉𝑃𝑃,0 ) sell at a higher price, and have a yield to
      maturity lower than similar vanilla bonds (𝑉𝑉𝑁𝑁𝑃𝑃,0 ):
                            𝑉𝑉𝑃𝑃,0 = 𝑉𝑉𝑁𝑁𝑃𝑃,0 + 𝑶𝑶𝑶𝑶𝑶𝑶𝟎𝟎
    • As for callable bonds, puttable bonds can be evaluated using a binomial
      tree.
    • The principle is exactly the same, but reversed (i.e. when the value of
      discounted cash-flows falls below the exercise price, we replace the value
      at that node with the strike price).
    • For instance, in the high state of the 1 time-step we would take
                                         𝑽𝑽       +𝐶𝐶              𝑽𝑽𝟐𝟐,𝑯𝑯𝑯𝑯 +𝐶𝐶
                       𝑽𝑽𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯 = max(𝑝𝑝 𝟐𝟐,𝑯𝑯𝑯𝑯         + 1 − 𝑝𝑝                   ; PP)
                                          1+𝒓𝒓𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯                 1+𝒓𝒓𝟏𝟏,𝑯𝑯
